title: Acute confusion in an elderly woman

chart.oneLiner: 80F from a nursing facility is brought in with one day of acute confusion, decreased oral intake, and fever.

opening: I want to go home… is it morning?
patientFile:
PERSONA: 80-year-old woman, intermittently confused, fluctuating attention, a poor historian. Mild distress.

COLLATERAL (provide ONLY if the student asks facility staff/family/chart/medication list): Nursing facility reports she was at her baseline (alert, oriented) until yesterday, when she became confused, lethargic, and ate/drank little. Noted to have foul-smelling urine and urinary frequency over 2 days. No falls, no new medications. History of recurrent UTIs. MEDS: amlodipine, donepezil (baseline mild dementia but oriented at baseline).

HPI (from patient — limited): can't give a clear history; says she 'feels off.' Denies clear pain when asked.
PMH: hypertension, mild dementia, recurrent UTIs. SOCIAL: lives in assisted facility.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: confused, fluctuating attention, dry mucous membranes.
- Vitals: BP 98/58, HR 112, RR 22, 38.7°C, SpO2 95% RA.
- Abdomen: mild suprapubic tenderness; possible left CVA tenderness if examined.
- Neuro: no focal motor/sensory deficit; disoriented; no neck stiffness.
- Lungs: clear. Cardiac: tachycardic, regular.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis.
- The patient is confused: vague/fluctuating answers. Provide COLLATERAL only when the student explicitly asks facility/family/chart/med list.
- Suprapubic/CVA tenderness and dry membranes are revealed only if examined.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.

labs.UA: Cloudy; large leukocyte esterase, positive nitrites, many WBCs and bacteria.
labs.CBC: WBC 17.5 with left shift, Hgb 12.0, Plt 240.
labs.BMP: Na 144, K 4.0, BUN 34, Cr 1.6 (baseline 0.9 — AKI), Glu 110.
labs.Cultures: Blood and urine cultures drawn; pending.

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Beat-to-beat alternation of QRS amplitude with low voltage and sinus tachycardia.
ekg.verify: Source a study matching: Beat-to-beat alternation of QRS amplitude with low voltage and sinus tachycardia. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.

ekg.clinicalVignette: A 72-year-old woman presents with fever, lethargy, and racing heart, and her tracing shows beat-to-beat QRS swings.

cxr.label: Chest X-ray
cxr.findings: No consolidation or effusion (obtained to exclude pneumonia as the infectious source).
cxr.verify: Pick a CC normal PA chest film.
cxr.imageDescription: No consolidation or effusion (obtained to exclude pneumonia as the infectious source).
searchTerms: normal PA chest radiograph no consolidation effusion
cxr.clinicalVignette: A 72-year-old woman with fever and delirium has a film obtained to exclude pneumonia as the infectious source.

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your assessment, list your differential for this acute confusion (note your leading diagnosis).
steps.rubric: Delirium due to UTI/urosepsis (leading,7). Hypoglycemia (CANT-MISS,4). Stroke/intracranial process (CANT-MISS,4). Pneumonia/other sepsis source (core,3). Medication/metabolic causes (core,3). Meningitis (CANT-MISS to consider,2). Named leading dx w/ justification — acute change from baseline, fever, urinary symptoms (core,2).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Delirium due to UTI/urosepsis. Must-not-miss diagnoses to exclude: Hypoglycemia; Stroke/intracranial process; Meningitis. Also reasonable: Pneumonia/other sepsis source; Medication/metabolic causes.

coreActions.item: Named leading dx w/ justification — acute change from baseline, fever, urinary symptoms

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial diagnostic workup do you want to order?
steps.rubric: Fingerstick glucose (CANT-MISS,3). Urinalysis + urine culture (CANT-MISS,4). CBC (2). BMP (2). Lactate (2). Blood cultures (1). CXR (1). CT head if focal deficit (bonus,1). PENALTY: starting antibiotics with no source workup or anchoring without checking glucose (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: EKG; CXR; Glucose; Urinalysis; CBC; BMP; Lactate; Cultures.

penalties.item: starting antibiotics with no source workup or anchoring without checking glucose

contraindicatedTests: starting antibiotics with no source workup or anchoring without checking glucose
orderSensitiveActions: starting antibiotics with no source workup or anchoring without checking glucose

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = electrical alternans with low voltage and tachycardia. CREDIT: recognizes electrical alternans / beat-to-beat QRS amplitude alternation (7); notes low voltage (5); links alternans + low voltage + tachycardia to a large pericardial effusion/tamponade (3). PENALTY: calling the study normal sinus tachycardia / missing the alternans (-3).
keyFindings: links alternans + low voltage + tachycardia to pericardial effusion/tamponade
criticalFindings: Electrical alternans with low voltage (suggests large pericardial effusion).
steps.idealAnswer: Electrical alternans: alternating QRS amplitude with low voltage and tachycardia, suggesting a large pericardial effusion.

criticalActions: Electrical alternans with low voltage (suggests large pericardial effusion).
coreActions: links alternans + low voltage + tachycardia to pericardial effusion/tamponade

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the abnormality

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = no consolidation/effusion. CREDIT: identifies a normal film (6); notes it excludes pneumonia as the source (4). PENALTY: inventing pathology (-2).
keyFindings: notes it excludes pneumonia as the source
criticalFindings: No consolidation or effusion (obtained to exclude pneumonia as the infectious source).
steps.idealAnswer: No consolidation or effusion (obtained to exclude pneumonia as the infectious source).

criticalActions.item: Recognizes a normal film: no consolidation or effusion (obtained to exclude pneumonia as the infectious source).

coreActions.item: notes it excludes pneumonia as the source

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: Given the pyuria/bacteriuria, leukocytosis, elevated lactate, and AKI, what is your diagnosis?
steps.rubric: Urosepsis causing delirium (6). Recognizes sepsis physiology — hypotension, tachycardia, elevated lactate, AKI (3). Notes delirium is multifactorial in the elderly (bonus,1).
steps.idealAnswer: Urosepsis with delirium: pyuria/bacteriuria localize the source to the urinary tract, and the leukocytosis, elevated lactate, hypotension/tachycardia, and AKI reflect sepsis physiology with end-organ hypoperfusion. Delirium is multifactorial in the elderly but is driven here by the infection.

bonusActions.item: Notes delirium is multifactorial in the elderly

steps.label: Management
steps.rubric: Per MGH sepsis (Surviving Sepsis): cultures then empiric broad-spectrum IV antibiotics within 1 h (CANT-MISS,7); IV crystalloid resuscitation 30 mL/kg (CANT-MISS,5); norepinephrine first-line for MAP<65 after fluids (4); source control – relieve obstruction/remove line (3); serial lactate (2); treat delirium non-pharmacologically (2). PENALTY: delaying antibiotics for tests (-4); dopamine as first-line pressor (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Urosepsis: obtain blood and urine cultures, then give empiric broad-spectrum IV antibiotics within 1 hour (e.g. ceftriaxone; cover resistant GNR if risk). 30 mL/kg balanced crystalloid for hypoperfusion. Norepinephrine first-line if MAP <65 after fluids. Source control (relieve obstruction, remove catheter). Serial lactate. Treat delirium non-pharmacologically.

coreActions.item: Blood and urine cultures before antibiotics

optionalActions: Blood and urine cultures before antibiotics

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 80F from a nursing facility is brought in with one day of acute confusion, decreased oral intake, and fever. The diagnosis is Urosepsis with delirium, reached by eliciting the focused history, performing the targeted exam, and interpreting the directed workup while excluding the must-not-miss alternatives.
keyPhysicalExamFindings: Abdomen: mild suprapubic tenderness; possible left CVA tenderness if examined.
Abdomen: mild suprapubic tenderness; possible left CVA tenderness if examined.
Neuro: no focal motor/sensory deficit; disoriented; no neck stiffness.
Neuro: no focal motor/sensory deficit; disoriented; no neck stiffness.
General: confused, fluctuating attention, dry mucous membranes.
General: confused, fluctuating attention, dry mucous membranes.
reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals.
Elicit the focused history, including sensitive items only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam maneuvers to reveal key findings.
Build a differential led by Urosepsis with delirium while explicitly excluding must-not-miss causes.
Order a focused, order-sensitive workup and interpret EKG/CXR and labs.
Commit to Urosepsis with delirium and manage with stabilization, definitive treatment, consults, and disposition.

imageInterpretationPearls: An incidental abnormality is an interpretation task in its own right — name it, then judge its relevance.
Read every study systematically before committing.
State pertinent negatives, not just positives.

items.item: Shows empathy for the patient's concern
Explains next steps in patient-friendly language
